title: The Deal Edges Closer: Pakistan's Mediation Push and NATO's Identity Crisis
subhead: US-Iran talks show 'slight progress' as Pakistan's army chief heads to Tehran, while NATO allies face confusion over American troop cuts and Europe confronts the reality of higher energy prices through 2027.

sections.heading: Pakistan's Mediation Intensifies as Tehran Weighs US Proposal
sections.body:
Pakistan's Army Chief Field Marshal Asim Munir departed for Tehran on Friday, the second visit in just over a month, as Islamabad ramps up efforts to broker a peace deal between the US and Iran. Pakistani Interior Minister Mohsin Naqvi has been in Tehran since Wednesday, meeting twice with Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi to review proposals for ending the war.

US Secretary of State Marco Rubio acknowledged 'slight progress' in talks with Iran on Friday, though he cautioned against exaggerating the movement. 'I don't want to exaggerate it, but there's been a little bit of movement, and that's good,' Rubio told reporters during NATO meetings in Sweden. The cautious optimism follows President Trump's statement that he was holding off on a military strike because 'serious negotiations' were underway.

UAE presidential advisor Anwar Gargash assessed the odds of a deal at '50-50,' warning that any settlement must address root causes of instability. The central sticking point remains the Strait of Hormuz, where Iran has established what it calls a 'tolling system' for ship transits — a mechanism Rubio explicitly rejected in Friday's comments.
sections.whyItMatters: Pakistan has emerged as the critical mediator in the US-Iran conflict, leveraging its relationships with both Tehran and Washington. The intensity of Pakistani diplomatic engagement — with both the interior minister and army chief in Tehran simultaneously — signals that negotiations have moved beyond procedural discussions into substantive territory. Whether Tehran accepts US terms on Hormuz access will determine if the ceasefire holds or the war resumes.

sections.heading: EU Sanctions Iran Over Hormuz Blockade as Oil Prices Climb
sections.body:
European Union nations moved Friday toward imposing sanctions on Iranian officials responsible for blocking the Strait of Hormuz, deeming the blockade 'contrary to international law.' The technical step extends the scope of existing Iran sanctions regimes, allowing for travel bans and asset freezes on individuals involved in disrupting freedom of navigation.

Oil prices rose Friday as investors doubted the prospects of a breakthrough in peace talks. Market uncertainty persists despite diplomatic signals, with buyers also preparing for the US summer driving season. Prices remain on track for a weekly loss, reflecting the volatile calculus of war and diplomacy.

US Central Command reported that American forces have redirected 97 commercial vessels since imposing a military blockade on Iranian ports and ships in mid-April, with four vessels disabled for non-compliance. The dual blockade — Iran at Hormuz, the US at Iranian ports — continues to squeeze global energy flows.
sections.whyItMatters: The EU sanctions move formalizes Europe's alignment with US pressure on Iran, though the economic burden falls heavily on European consumers. ECB President Christine Lagarde confirmed that even if the conflict ended now, 'lagging effects' would keep prices elevated through 2027, with inflation forecast at 3.1% this year — significantly higher than earlier projections of 1.9%.

sections.heading: NATO Allies Confront American Troop Cuts and Communication Chaos
sections.body:
Secretary of State Rubio told NATO allies in Sweden to 'face up' to upcoming US troop cuts in Europe, acknowledging that while allies are 'aware' of the adjustments, they won't be 'thrilled about it.' The meeting in Helsingborg was marked by confusion after Trump announced 5,000 troops would go to Poland — reversing an earlier decision to cancel that deployment — just weeks after abruptly announcing the withdrawal of 5,000 troops from Germany.

Swedish Foreign Minister Maria Malmer Stenergard called the situation 'confusing indeed, and not always easy to navigate.' Norwegian FM Espen Barth Eide stressed the importance of structured drawdowns so 'Europe is able to build up when the US reduces its presence.'

The diplomatic friction comes after Trump criticized European allies for refusing to support his Iran war and threatened to consider leaving NATO. Rubio reiterated Trump's 'disappointment' with allies and said it would 'have to be addressed.' European nations have dispatched vessels toward the Gulf to help with Hormuz operations when the war ends.
sections.whyItMatters: NATO's July summit in Ankara was supposed to showcase European defense spending increases — commitments to reach 5% of GDP on defense. Instead, allies are struggling to coordinate with a US administration that sends mixed signals on troop deployments. France's FM Jean-Noel Barrot called for 'Europeanising NATO,' reflecting growing recognition that Europe must shoulder more of its own security burden. The Iran war has exposed how dependent European energy security remains on US military power.

sections.heading: Israel-Lebanon Tensions Persist Despite Ceasefire Framework
sections.body:
More than 3,100 people have been killed by Israeli attacks in Lebanon since March 2, according to Lebanon's health ministry, with 9,432 injured. An Israeli strike on Friday killed six people in southern Lebanon, including two rescuers and a child. Attacks continue despite a US-brokered ceasefire announced in mid-April, which Hezbollah has rejected.

Lebanon and Israel are preparing for a fourth round of peace talks in Washington in June, with Lebanon forming a military delegation for security discussions at the Pentagon on May 29. The US sanctioned a group of Hezbollah-affiliated parliamentarians and security officials Thursday, including Iran's ambassador to Lebanon, for obstructing disarmament efforts.

The Lebanese army issued a statement affirming that all officers remain loyal to the institution after the US sanctions included, for the first time, an army officer accused of sharing information with Hezbollah.
sections.whyItMatters: The Lebanon-Israel front remains volatile even as diplomatic frameworks take shape. Hezbollah's rejection of the ceasefire creates a two-track reality: official Lebanese government participation in talks while the militia continues operations. The death toll climbing past 3,100 adds pressure on all sides. The US sanctions on a Lebanese army officer mark a new escalation, testing the military's institutional cohesion at a critical moment.

sections.heading: Russia-China Summit Signals Deepening Alignment
sections.body:
Vladimir Putin's visit to Beijing on May 19-20, coming just days after Trump's own trip to China, delivered a clear political message: Western pressure has not isolated Russia. Xi Jinping hailed a 'new stage' in China-Russia relations, with joint statements criticizing US missile defense plans and warning against a return to the 'law of the jungle.'

Behind the ceremony, however, the partnership's limits remain visible. Russia and China failed to finalize the Power of Siberia 2 pipeline deal, which would carry 50 billion cubic meters of gas annually through Mongolia. Gazprom shares fell after the summit produced no breakthrough and no dividend for 2025.

For India, the deepening Russia-China alignment poses a strategic dilemma. Moscow's growing dependence on Beijing could affect defense technology transfers and energy pricing for New Delhi. India's 2026 BRICS chairmanship becomes a platform to define multipolarity in its own terms — not simply as anti-US alignment.
sections.whyItMatters: The Russia-China relationship is becoming more institutionalized but remains asymmetrical. China holds the stronger hand: Russia needs Chinese markets, technology, and finance far more than China needs Russia. Yet the partnership serves both: Moscow gains economic lifelines and diplomatic cover, while Beijing acquires discounted energy and a partner willing to challenge US influence. The missing pipeline deal shows that Beijing negotiates hard even with 'friends.'

bottomLine: Three months into the US-Iran war, the diplomatic track shows signs of life but no breakthrough. Pakistan's intensive mediation — now involving both the interior minister and army chief — signals that substantive negotiations are underway. Rubio's 'slight progress' and Gargash's '50-50' odds suggest the gap between US and Iranian positions has narrowed, though whether Tehran accepts terms on Hormuz remains uncertain. Meanwhile, the economic and alliance fallout continues: EU inflation projections revised sharply upward, oil prices volatile on deal skepticism, and NATO allies struggling to parse contradictory signals from Washington on troop deployments. The Russia-China summit in Beijing confirmed a deepening partnership built on shared grievances but limited by Chinese leverage over Moscow. For all the diplomatic movement, nothing has fundamentally changed the structure of the standoff — Iran still holds Hormuz, the US maintains its blockade, and the question remains whether both sides can find a face-saving formula to de-escalate.
